patterns.what_to_look_for: API/serializer returns the full ORM entity instead of a vetted field allowlist, leaking internal/sensitive columns (password_hash, mfa_secret, is_admin, internal_notes, other users' emails) the UI never displays.
source_indicators: res.json(entity) or returning ORM object directly (.lean(), .toObject(), Model.findById)
DRF serializer with fields = '__all__' or no Meta.fields allowlist
Jackson/Gson serializing a whole JPA entity without @JsonIgnore on sensitive fields
GraphQL type/resolver exposing hashedPassword/secret/internal fields of the row
SELECT * feeding the response body; no Prisma/Sequelize select/attributes projection
blackbox_indicators: JSON carries fields absent from the rendered UI (passwordHash, ssn, isAdmin, createdByIp, internalNotes)
diff between mobile/API JSON and the web page reveals extra PII
nested objects include full related-user records
framework leftovers present (__v, _id, deletedAt, role flags)
evidence_required: raw HTTP response showing the sensitive fields
the request (method/path/auth context) that returned them
mapping showing the fields are not used/rendered by the client
classification of each leaked field (PII / secret / authz-relevant)
false_positive_checks: field is encrypted/tokenized rather than plaintext
field is the caller's own data, not another user's
response is post-filtered by a gateway/BFF before the client
value is a non-reversible hash with no auth value
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Authenticated GET the object/list endpoint and grep the JSON for fields not shown in the UI (password, secret, ssn, is_admin, other users' email).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as Excessive Data Exposure (OWASP API3): list the exact leaked fields, whose data they are, and the response that returned them.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Sensitive data (passwords, tokens, session IDs, card/SSN, full request bodies, Authorization/Cookie headers) written to application logs, log aggregation, or third-party telemetry without scrubbing.
source_indicators: logger.info/debug(req.body) or logging the whole request/response object
console.log(user) or logging Authorization / Cookie / Set-Cookie headers
logging full SQL with bound parameters, or exceptions that include the input payload
Sentry/Datadog/analytics capture with no body/header scrubbing
morgan/access-log format that includes query strings carrying tokens
blackbox_indicators: tokens/session IDs/PII appear in URL query strings (they land in access logs)
error responses echo the same data that would be logged
debug/request-id headers reflect submitted input
evidence_required: a log line or telemetry event containing the sensitive value
source location of the log statement
classification of the data plus who can read that log sink
false_positive_checks: value already masked/redacted (****1234, [REDACTED])
log statement only fires at a debug level disabled in prod (confirm prod config)
sink is access-controlled with short retention (still report, lower severity)
value is a public identifier, not a secret/PII
patterns.suggested_validation_task: If log/telemetry access exists, grep for known secret/PII test values submitted in a prior request; otherwise confirm the log statement and prod log level in source.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as PII/secret in logs: cite the log statement, the sink, its access/retention, and a masked sample of the leaked line.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Error responses leak stack traces, SQL/exception messages, framework versions, file paths, env vars, or echoed input, exposing internals and sometimes other users' data.
source_indicators: Express error handler returning err.stack / err.message to the client
Django DEBUG=True, Flask debug=True, Rails consider_all_requests_local=true
ASP.NET customErrors=Off / detailed errors enabled
raw DB driver error returned in the response (err.detail, ORA-, PG/MySQL error)
no try/catch around handlers so the default 500 dumps a trace
blackbox_indicators: 500 body contains a stack trace, file paths, or line numbers
SQL error reveals table and column names
framework debug page (Werkzeug, Whoops, Symfony profiler)
exception message echoes submitted input; verbose Server/X-Powered-By headers
evidence_required: HTTP response showing the leaked internals
the request (often malformed/edge input) that triggered it
what was disclosed (paths, schema, versions, secrets, other-user data)
false_positive_checks: generic error page with no internal detail (only a request ID)
stack present only in a non-prod environment
disclosed paths/versions are already public knowledge
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send benign malformed input (bad type, oversized field, broken JSON) to a few endpoints and inspect 4xx/5xx bodies for stack traces or DB errors.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as Verbose Error / Information Disclosure: include the trigger request and the exact internals disclosed; flag if any other-user data leaked.

patterns.what_to_look_for: List, search, autocomplete, export, or relation-include endpoints return records or fields belonging to other users/tenants because the query isn't scoped to the caller, even when the single-object endpoint is properly guarded.
source_indicators: Model.findAll/findMany on a list route with no where userId/tenantId/orgId filter
search/autocomplete querying all rows and returning name/email/phone
ORM include/populate/with of a sensitive relation without scoping
GraphQL nested resolver fetching related entities without per-field authz
report/export builder whose query ignores caller scope; ?include=/?expand=/?fields= expanding into other-owner data
blackbox_indicators: search/autocomplete returns other users' emails or phones
list endpoint returns more rows than the UI shows for this account
adding ?include=owner / ?expand=user reveals other-user PII
total/count in the response exceeds the caller's own data; GraphQL nesting surfaces foreign records
evidence_required: two-account proof: account A's response containing account B's data
the exact request and the leaked foreign-owned fields
confirmation the data is not an intentional public directory
false_positive_checks: data is an intentional public directory/social graph
results are de-identified (opaque IDs only, no PII)
scoping happens in a downstream BFF/gateway not in the reviewed service
the 'foreign' record is actually shared with the caller via membership
patterns.suggested_validation_task: From a low-privilege account hit list/search/export endpoints (and add ?include=/?expand=) and check whether any returned record or field belongs to another account.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as IDOR-adjacent excessive exposure: show A seeing B's data via the collection/relation path, even when single-object GET is secured.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Secrets or PII placed in URL query strings (reset/API tokens, session IDs, emails) or authenticated PII responses served as cacheable, leaking via browser cache, shared proxies, Referer headers, and access logs.
source_indicators: redirect/link building a token or session into a query string (?token=, ?sid=, ?api_key=)
GET routes accepting password/token/PII as query parameters
auth'd PII endpoint returning Cache-Control: public / max-age, or missing no-store
missing Referrer-Policy on pages that link off-site while holding a token in the URL
password-reset/magic-link delivered as a long-lived, non-one-time GET token
blackbox_indicators: tokens/session IDs/emails visible in the URL query string
Cache-Control: public or no Cache-Control: no-store on authenticated PII responses
no Referrer-Policy header on token-bearing pages
ETag/Last-Modified caching on per-user PII; third-party scripts loaded on a token-bearing URL
evidence_required: the URL or response headers showing the secret/PII or the cacheable directive
demonstration of a leak vector (Referer sent off-site, or a cached copy retrievable)
classification of what is exposed plus its lifetime/reuse
false_positive_checks: token is single-use and invalidated immediately (lower impact)
response actually carries no-store/private despite a nearby public asset
value in the URL is a non-sensitive public ID
page makes no cross-origin requests, so the Referer leak is moot
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Inspect token-bearing URLs and an authenticated PII page's response headers for missing Cache-Control: no-store / Referrer-Policy; check if back-button or a logged-out replay serves cached PII.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as Sensitive Data in URL/Cache: name the secret/PII, the leak channel (logs/Referer/cache), and the missing header.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Server embeds more data than the user needs into HTML/JS, inline JSON state, hidden form fields, HTML comments, or deployed source maps, leaking other users' records, secrets, or internal fields to view-source.
source_indicators: SSR dumping a full store/entity into window.__INITIAL_STATE__ / __NEXT_DATA__ / __APOLLO_STATE__
templates rendering admin/other-user fields into hidden inputs or data-* attributes
source maps (*.js.map) emitted to the production build/CDN
secrets/PII hardcoded or inlined into the client bundle/config
GraphQL/REST response with extra fields cached into client state
blackbox_indicators: view-source reveals inline JSON with foreign-user records, emails, or secret flags
*.js.map fetchable from the asset host (reveals source and comments)
hidden inputs / data-* attributes containing PII or authz flags
HTML comments with internal info (paths, names, TODO creds); __NEXT_DATA__/__INITIAL_STATE__ wider than the current user's scope
evidence_required: the page source or .map URL showing the embedded sensitive data
identification of whose data or which secret is exposed
confirmation it reaches the browser without authorization to that data
false_positive_checks: embedded data is the current user's own, intended for hydration
.map returns 404 or is access-restricted
values are public config (publishable keys), not secrets
hidden field is an opaque CSRF/nonce token, not PII
patterns.suggested_validation_task: View page source and fetch the matching *.js.map for inline JSON state, hidden fields, or comments containing other-user PII or secrets.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as Client-Side Data Exposure: quote the inline state/map/hidden field and identify the foreign data or secret leaked to the browser.
